title: Classical probability — fair 20-faced die
prompt:
Rolling a fair die with **20 faces** (numbered 1–20), what is the probability of getting a **3**?

The size of the sample space $\#S$ and the number of favourable events $\#E$ are given. Compute the probability $P=\#E/\#S$ in cell **C6**.

given.A1: Fair 20-faced die — P(getting a 3)

checks.label: Probability P
checks.teach: Classical (Laplace) probability is the ratio of favourable to possible equally-likely outcomes: $P=\#E/\#S=1/20=0.05$ (5%).

method: Every face is equally likely, so $P=\dfrac{\#E}{\#S}=\dfrac{1}{20}=0.05$.

title: Classical probability — defective pieces
prompt:
A batch of **500 pieces** contains **8 defective** ones. In a procedure that tests a single piece, what is the probability of picking a defective one?

Fill $\#S$ in **C3**, $\#E$ in **C4**, then the probability in **C6**.

given.A1: Batch of 500 pieces, 8 defective — P(defective)

checks.label: Sample-space size #S
checks.teach: Any of the 500 pieces can be tested, so $\#S=500$.

checks.label: Favourable count #E
checks.teach: There are 8 defective pieces, so $\#E=8$.

title: Classical probability — sum of two dice
prompt:
Rolling **two** fair six-sided dice, what is the probability that the two numbers have **sum 5**?

There are $6\times 6=36$ equally-likely couples. **Count** the favourable couples (sum = 5) into **C4**, then compute the probability in **C6**.

given.A1: Two dice — P(sum = 5)

checks.label: Favourable couples #E
checks.teach: The couples giving 5 are (1,4), (2,3), (3,2), (4,1): four of them.

checks.label: Probability P
checks.teach: $P=4/36=0.1\overline{1}\approx 0.111$ (about 11.1%).

title: Binomial distribution — BINOM.DIST
prompt:
A machine produces items independently with a defect probability $p=0.1$. A sample of $n=10$ items is taken. Use Excel's **BINOM.DIST** to find the probabilities below.

Recall **BINOM.DIST(k; n; p; cumulative)**: the last argument is **0/FALSE** for $P(X=k)$ and **1/TRUE** for $P(X\le k)$.

given.A1: Binomial: n independent trials, defect prob. p

checks.label: P(X = 2)
checks.teach: Exact probability uses cumulative = 0 (FALSE): $\binom{10}{2}0.1^2 0.9^8 = 0.1937$.

checks.label: P(X ≤ 2)
checks.teach: At-most uses cumulative = 1 (TRUE): it sums $P(0)+P(1)+P(2)=0.9298$.

checks.label: P(X ≥ 1)
checks.teach: ‘At least one’ is the complement of ‘none’: $1-P(X=0)=1-0.9^{10}=0.6513$.

title: Hypergeometric distribution — HYPGEOM.DIST
prompt:
A box contains **M = 50** items, of which **C = 8** are defective. You draw **n = 5** items **without replacement** (so trials are not independent → hypergeometric, not binomial).

Use **HYPGEOM.DIST(k; n; C; M; cumulative)** to find the probabilities below.

given.A1: Hypergeometric: draw n from M without replacement

checks.label: P(X = 2)
checks.teach: Exact hypergeometric probability with cumulative = 0: $\binom{8}{2}\binom{42}{3}/\binom{50}{5}=0.1517$.

checks.label: P(X ≤ 1)
checks.teach: Cumulative = 1 sums $P(0)+P(1)=0.8241$. Without replacement the population shrinks each draw — that is why we cannot use BINOM.DIST here.

title: Classical probability — a 3 or a 5
prompt:
Rolling a fair die with **six faces** (numbered 1–6), what is the probability of getting **a 3 or a 5**?

The sample-space size $\#S$ is given. Count the favourable outcomes (3 or 5) into **C4**, then compute $P=\#E/\#S$ in **C6**.

given.A1: Fair six-faced die — P(3 or 5)

checks.label: Favourable count #E
checks.teach: The roll being a 3 or a 5 covers exactly two of the six faces, so $\#E=2$.

checks.label: Probability P
checks.teach: ‘3 or 5’ are mutually exclusive outcomes, so probabilities add: $P=\dfrac{\#E}{\#S}=\dfrac{2}{6}=0.3\overline{3}\approx 33.3\%$.

method: The two faces 3 and 5 are mutually exclusive, so $P(3\cup 5)=P(3)+P(5)=\tfrac16+\tfrac16=\tfrac26=0.3\overline{3}$.

title: Classical probability — overlapping defects (inclusion–exclusion)
prompt:
In a batch of **500 pieces**, **4** have defect **A**, **3** have defect **B**, and **2** of these have **both** defects. Examining a single piece, what is the probability it is **defective**?

A piece is defective if it has A *or* B. Because 2 pieces are counted twice, use inclusion–exclusion $\#E = n_A+n_B-n_{AB}$ in **C7**, then $P=\#E/\#S$ in **C9**.

given.A1: Batch of 500 — defect A=4, B=3, both=2 — P(defective)

checks.label: Defective count #E
checks.teach: By inclusion–exclusion $\#E=n_A+n_B-n_{AB}=4+3-2=5$. We subtract the 2 'both' pieces once because adding $n_A$ and $n_B$ counts them twice.

checks.label: Probability P
checks.teach: $P=\dfrac{\#E}{\#S}=\dfrac{5}{500}=0.01$ (1%). Forgetting to subtract the overlap would wrongly give $7/500$.

title: Probability tree — a 3 then a 5 (two rolls)
prompt:
Rolling a fair six-sided die **twice**, what is the probability of getting **3 on the first** roll **and 5 on the second**?

The two rolls are **independent**, so multiply the branch probabilities along the path: $P=P_1\times P_2$ in **C6**.

given.A1: Two rolls — P(3 then 5)

checks.label: P(3 then 5)
checks.teach: Independent actions multiply along a tree path: $P=\tfrac16\times\tfrac16=\tfrac1{36}\approx 0.0278$ (about 2.8%).

method: One specific path through the tree: $P=P_1\,P_2=\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac16=\tfrac1{36}$.

title: Probability tree — a 3 and a 5 in any order
prompt:
Rolling a fair six-sided die **twice**, what is the probability of getting **a 3 and a 5** (in **either** order)?

There are **two** favourable paths: (3 then 5) and (5 then 3). Each path has probability $\tfrac16\times\tfrac16$. Sum them in **C6**.

given.A1: Two rolls — P(a 3 and a 5, any order)

checks.label: P(3 and 5, any order)
checks.teach: Two mutually-exclusive paths, each $\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac16$: $P=2\times\tfrac1{36}=\tfrac2{36}\approx 0.0556$ (about 5.6%). Order matters in the tree, so '3 then 5' and '5 then 3' are counted separately.

title: Probability tree — exactly two 3s in three rolls (binomial)
prompt:
Rolling a fair six-sided die **three times**, what is the probability of getting **exactly two 3s**?

Each roll is an independent trial with 'success' = rolling a 3, so $p=\tfrac16$. This is the **binomial** scheme: use **BINOM.DIST(k; n; p; 0)** with $k=2$, $n=3$ in **C7**.

given.A1: Three rolls — P(exactly two 3s)

checks.label: P(exactly two 3s)
checks.teach: $P=\binom{3}{2}\left(\tfrac16\right)^2\left(\tfrac56\right)^1=3\cdot\tfrac1{36}\cdot\tfrac56=\tfrac{15}{216}\approx 0.0694$. The factor $\binom32=3$ counts the three tree paths with two 3s and one non-3 — exactly what the tree diagram enumerates.

method: Binomial with $n=3,\,p=\tfrac16,\,k=2$: $\binom32 p^2(1-p)=3\cdot\tfrac1{36}\cdot\tfrac56=0.0694$. The tree's three favourable paths are the $\binom32$ term.

title: Probability tree — exactly two heads in three coin tosses
prompt:
Tossing a fair coin **three times**, what is the probability of getting **exactly two heads**?

Binomial scheme with $n=3$, $p=\tfrac12$. Use **BINOM.DIST(k; n; p; 0)** with $k=2$ in **C7**.

given.A1: Three coin tosses — P(exactly two heads)

checks.label: P(exactly two heads)
checks.teach: $P=\binom32\left(\tfrac12\right)^2\left(\tfrac12\right)^1=3\cdot\tfrac18=\tfrac38=0.375$. Of the 8 equally-likely HHH…TTT outcomes, exactly 3 (HHT, HTH, THH) have two heads.

title: Probability tree — at least two heads in three tosses
prompt:
Tossing a fair coin **three times**, what is the probability of getting **at least two heads**?

'At least two' means $k=2$ or $k=3$. Use the cumulative complement: $P(X\ge 2)=1-P(X\le 1)$ via **BINOM.DIST** in **C7**.

given.A1: Three coin tosses — P(at least two heads)

checks.label: P(at least two heads)
checks.teach: $P(X\ge2)=1-P(X\le1)=1-[P(0)+P(1)]=1-[\tfrac18+\tfrac38]=\tfrac48=0.5$. Equivalently $P(2)+P(3)=\tfrac38+\tfrac18=\tfrac12$.

title: Probability tree — at most two heads in three tosses
prompt:
Tossing a fair coin **three times**, what is the probability of getting **at most two heads**?

'At most two' is the cumulative $P(X\le 2)$. Use **BINOM.DIST(2; n; p; 1)** in **C7**.

given.A1: Three coin tosses — P(at most two heads)

checks.label: P(at most two heads)
checks.teach: $P(X\le2)=1-P(X=3)=1-\tfrac18=\tfrac78=0.875$. The only excluded outcome is HHH (all three heads).

title: Probability tree — exactly one defective in two draws (no replacement)
prompt:
A batch of **500 pieces** contains **5 defective** ones (4 with defect A, 2 with defect B, 1 with both → $4+2-1=5$ distinct defectives). Two pieces are examined **without replacement**. What is the probability of finding **exactly one** defective?

Two paths give exactly one defective: (defective, good) and (good, defective). After the first draw the batch holds 499 pieces. Compute $P=2\cdot\tfrac{5}{500}\cdot\tfrac{495}{499}$ in **C8**.

given.A1: Two draws, no replacement — P(exactly one defective)

checks.label: P(exactly one defective)
checks.teach: Two ordered paths each have probability $\tfrac{5}{500}\cdot\tfrac{495}{499}$ (the bad one, then a good one from the remaining 499). Summing: $P=2\cdot\tfrac{5}{500}\cdot\tfrac{495}{499}\approx0.01984$. Drawing without replacement is why the second factor uses 499, not 500.

title: Probability tree — at least one defective in two draws (complement)
prompt:
Same batch of **500 pieces** with **5 defective**. Two pieces are examined **without replacement**. What is the probability of finding **at least one** defective?

Use the complement 'no defective in either draw': $P=1-\tfrac{495}{500}\cdot\tfrac{494}{499}$ in **C8**.

given.A1: Two draws, no replacement — P(at least one defective)

checks.label: P(at least one defective)
checks.teach: ‘At least one’ is the complement of ‘none’: $P=1-\tfrac{495}{500}\cdot\tfrac{494}{499}\approx0.01992$. After drawing one good piece, 494 of the remaining 499 are still good.

title: Probability tree — at least one defective in three draws (complement)
prompt:
Same batch of **500 pieces** with **5 defective**. **Three** pieces are examined **without replacement**. What is the probability of finding **at least one** defective?

Complement of 'all three good': $P=1-\tfrac{495}{500}\cdot\tfrac{494}{499}\cdot\tfrac{493}{498}$ in **C8**.

given.A1: Three draws, no replacement — P(at least one defective)

checks.label: P(at least one defective)
checks.teach: Complement of 'three good in a row': $P=1-\tfrac{495}{500}\cdot\tfrac{494}{499}\cdot\tfrac{493}{498}\approx0.02976$. Each successive draw shrinks both the numerator (good left) and the denominator (pieces left) by one.

title: Total probability & Bayes — two overlapping defects (P01)
prompt:
Turned parts have a **diameter** defect with probability **1%**, a **shape** (taper) defect with probability **2%**, and **both** defects with probability **0.1%**.

- In **C8**: the **total** probability a part is defective, $P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)$.
- In **C9**: the probability that, **given** it is defective, this is due to **both** defects, $P(A\cap B \mid \text{def})=\tfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(A\cup B)}$.

given.A1: Turned parts — diameter / shape / both defects

checks.label: Total P(defective)
checks.teach: Inclusion–exclusion for the union: $P(A\cup B)=0.01+0.02-0.001=0.029$ (2.9%). Subtracting $P(A\cap B)$ avoids double-counting the parts with both defects.

checks.label: P(both | defective)
checks.teach: Conditional probability: $P(\text{both}\mid\text{def})=\dfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(A\cup B)}=\dfrac{0.001}{0.029}\approx0.0345$ (about 3.45%).

title: Total probability & Bayes — four subcontractors (P02)
prompt:
A producer receives components from four subcontractors A, B, C, D in proportions $P_a,P_b,P_c,P_d$, with declared defectivities $D_a,D_b,D_c,D_d$ (given).

- In **C7**: the probability of using a **defective** component (law of total probability), $\sum_i P_i D_i$.
- In **C8**: the probability that a defective component **came from B** (Bayes), $\dfrac{P_b D_b}{\sum_i P_i D_i}$.

given.A1: Four subcontractors — total probability & Bayes

checks.label: P(defective)
checks.teach: Law of total probability — weight each supplier's defectivity by its share: $0.65\cdot0.02+0.2\cdot0.025+0.1\cdot0.04+0.05\cdot0.1=0.027$ (2.7%).

checks.label: P(from B | defective)
checks.teach: Bayes: $P(B\mid\text{def})=\dfrac{P_b D_b}{P(\text{def})}=\dfrac{0.2\cdot0.025}{0.027}=\dfrac{0.005}{0.027}\approx0.1852$. Although B supplies only 20%, it contributes about 18.5% of the defectives.

method: Total: $\sum P_i D_i=0.027$. Bayes for B: $\tfrac{0.2\cdot0.025}{0.027}\approx0.1852$.

title: Union of three defects — conditional joints (P03)
prompt:
A semi-processed part can have three defects A, B, C. Given: $P(A)=0.03$, $P(B)=0.04$, $P(C)=0.02$. Among parts with A: $P(B\mid A)=0.30$, $P(C\mid A)=0.17$, $P(B\cap C\mid A)=0.03$. Among parts with B: $P(C\mid B)=0.18$.

The joint probabilities follow from the definition of conditional probability, e.g. $P(A\cap B)=P(A)\,P(B\mid A)$. Use the union of three sets
$$P(A\cup B\cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(AB)-P(AC)-P(BC)+P(ABC)$$
in **C11**.

given.A1: Three defects A, B, C — probability any defect is present

checks.label: P(any defect present)
checks.teach: Convert conditionals to joints first: $P(AB)=P(A)P(B\mid A)=0.03\cdot0.3$, $P(AC)=0.03\cdot0.17$, $P(BC)=P(B)P(C\mid B)=0.04\cdot0.18$, $P(ABC)=P(A)P(BC\mid A)=0.03\cdot0.03$. Then the inclusion–exclusion union gives $0.03+0.04+0.02-0.009-0.0051-0.0072+0.0009=0.0696$ (about 6.96%).

title: Series reliability — mechanism with three critical pieces (P04)
prompt:
A mechanism has three critical pieces A, B, C; it **fails if any one** is defective. Defect probabilities: $P_A=0.025$, $P_B=0.008$, $P_C=0.007$.

- In **C7**: the probability the mechanism **does not work**, $1-(1-P_A)(1-P_B)(1-P_C)$.
- In **C9**: the **maximum** acceptable $P_A$ so that the failure probability drops to the target $P_1=0.02$, i.e. $P_A^{\max}=1-\dfrac{1-P_1}{(1-P_B)(1-P_C)}$.

given.A1: Series mechanism — fails if any piece is defective
given.A9: Max acceptable PA for failure = P1 =

checks.label: P(does not work)
checks.teach: The mechanism works only if **all** pieces are good (independent): $P(\text{work})=(1-P_A)(1-P_B)(1-P_C)$. So $P(\text{fail})=1-(0.975)(0.992)(0.993)\approx0.03957$ (about 3.96%).

checks.label: Max acceptable PA
checks.teach: Set $P(\text{fail})=P_1$ and solve for $P_A$: $1-(1-P_A)(1-P_B)(1-P_C)=P_1\Rightarrow P_A^{\max}=1-\dfrac{1-P_1}{(1-P_B)(1-P_C)}\approx0.00513$. To hit 2% overall, piece A's defectivity must fall from 2.5% to about 0.51%.
